title: The Urban Green Lung
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
text: The rapid expansion of megacities has often come at the expense of natural landscapes, leading to what urbanists call 'concrete fatigue.' However, a growing body of research suggests that the integration of green spaces is not merely an aesthetic choice but a cognitive necessity. Critical thinking and creative problem-solving are significantly hampered by the constant 'directed attention' required to navigate a busy city environment—processing traffic lights, avoiding crowds, and filtering out noise. This mental fatigue is alleviated by exposure to natural settings, a process known as Attention Restoration Theory. Unlike the harsh stimuli of the city, nature provides 'soft fascination'—stimuli that capture attention without effort, allowing the brain's executive functions to replenish. Studies indicate that individuals who spend time in parks perform better on tasks requiring deep focus and logical reasoning than those who remain in purely built environments. Yet, the challenge remains for city planners who face immense pressure to prioritize high-density housing over public parks. The prevailing economic logic often overlooks the long-term public health and cognitive benefits of greenery. To advocate for more parks, one must apply the very critical thinking these spaces foster: analyzing data on mental well-being versus short-term real estate gains.

questions.question: What is the primary argument regarding 'green spaces' in the text?

options.A: They are primarily built to improve the visual appeal of a city.
options.B: They are essential for maintaining the cognitive health of urban residents.
options.C: They are more expensive to maintain than residential buildings.
options.D: They distract people from their daily professional responsibilities.

options.A: the physical degradation of old city buildings.
options.B: the psychological exhaustion caused by dense urban environments.
options.C: a lack of interest in modern architectural design.
options.D: the difficulty of commuting in large megacities.

questions.question: How does 'directed attention' affect the brain according to the writer?

options.A: It improves the ability to multitask effectively.
options.B: It drains mental energy by requiring constant focus on urban stimuli.
options.C: It helps individuals ignore distractions in the workplace.
options.D: It is the most efficient way to process complex information.

questions.question: What is the benefit of 'soft fascination'?

options.A: It forces the brain to work harder to understand nature.
options.B: It allows the mind to recover without demanding intense focus.
options.C: It helps people ignore the noise of the city more effectively.
options.D: It encourages people to move to rural areas permanently.

questions.question: The writer mentions city planners to highlight...

options.A: their success in creating sustainable modern cities.
options.B: the conflict between economic interests and public well-being.
options.C: the lack of training they receive in environmental science.
options.D: their preference for building more parks than necessary.

questions.question: What does the writer suggest is the most effective way to support the creation of parks?

options.A: By ignoring economic data and focusing on personal stories.
options.B: By using logical analysis to demonstrate their long-term value.
options.C: By protesting against the construction of new housing units.
options.D: By moving out of cities that do not provide enough greenery.
